title: Prof Jiang Was Right? Chinese Academic's Predictions Come True As US-Iran War Erupts | VERTEX
full_text: United States will go to war against Iran. And the third big prediction is that United States will lose this war. We can expect this war to last for many weeks, possibly also for many years. But I promise you that after this war is done, the world will never be the same again. So if the GCS collapses, the American economy and American empire both collapse at the same time. The war between [music] the United States and Iran is no longer just about missiles, drones, or naval fleets. It [music] is also about predictions. And one prediction made 2 years ago by an academic few people had heard of is suddenly going viral across the world. Imagine watching [music] a lecture in 2024 where a professor calmly says three things. First, Donald Trump will return to power. Second, the United States [music] will end up fighting Iran. and third, America will lose that war. At the time, many dismissed the idea [music] as speculation. But now, as the conflict between the United States, [music] Israel, and Iran intensifies, that lecture [music] is being watched again by millions. >> So, in this class, this semester, [music] I'm making three big predictions, right? First is that Trump will win uh in November. Second is [music] that um United States will go to war against Iran. And the third big prediction is that United States will lose this war which will forever change the global order. Um these [music] past uh so last class we looked at one [music] reason why the United States would invade Iran uh which is the Israel option. And in today's class, we'll look at another reason, which is the defense of empire. America is an [music] empire and it must defend its imperial status. >> The man behind the prediction is Professor Jang Schwekin. Jang is a Chinese Canadian historian, educator, and geopolitical [music] analyst known for his series predictive history where he uses historical patterns and game theory [music] to analyze global power struggles. He studied at Yale and later worked in journalism, education reform, and international development before becoming widely known online for his geopolitical forecasts. But it was one particular lecture that brought him global attention. In that talk, Jung argued that the United States might be drawn into a war with Iran under a Trump presidency. More controversially, he warned that such a war could end badly [music] for Washington. His argument was not based on ideology, but on history. In his latest video, Professor Jang [music] also raises a far more alarming possibility, warning that a direct US [music] ground invasion of Iran could draw other global powers into the conflict. >> Will will America send its army, half a million, 2 million soldiers to topple the government? Okay, that's question number one. Question number two, nuclear weapons. If you're losing a war, would you choose to use nuclear weapons? Okay, that's another big question that we have. Okay, another big question that we have is who gets involved? Because this situation is so dire that Europe, okay, specifically Germany, France, and Britain are talking about entering this war. And guess what? If that happens, it is possible that Russia and China will also enter this war on the side of the Iranians. This is World War II. Okay? Because of the importance of the political moves, because of the importance of the Middle East, everyone has to [music] get involved at some point. And we're going to use game theory to really try to understand what what's happening on the ground and where this could lead us. Okay, >> bye. >> Jang pointed out that great powers often struggle when fighting wars far from home [music] against determined regional powers with strong domestic support. In his analysis, Iran's geography, population size, and military resilience could make it extremely difficult for outside forces to achieve decisive [music] victory. Jang also suggested that Western assumptions about regime collapse inside Iran might be misplaced. According to Jang, one of the biggest miscalculations could be the belief that the Iranian public would turn against its government during a foreign attack. Instead, he predicted that external pressure might unite the country rather than divide it. >> What does United States want to do? What what what what does it want to do? By by doing what? And accomplishing what? It wants to topple the regime in in Iran, right? And you can only do that through a ground invasion. Okay? So the United States is trying to topple Iran. But then you also have other actors. [music] Okay? So, um, Iran, what does Iran want to do? What what what does Iran want to do? Well, they're basically the Revolutionary Guard Corp., [music] what do they want to do, right? They want to kill as many Americans as possible. Okay, [music] you understand? They want to force an American landing invasion. Okay? So Iran wants to force a US invasion knowing that if the US invades it has to lose the [music] war. Okay, it will lose the war because the re guard can just [music] send suicide bombers against United States. Okay, now let's look at Israel. We know that United States wants to topple Iran. In one part of the lecture, Professor Jung points to what he describes as powerful political forces in Washington that could push the United [music] States toward confrontation with Iran. >> So, um there are three major interests, forces that are pushing the United States towards war with Iran. Um the first is [music] the Israel lobby. Remember that the Israel lobby is composed of both Jewish interests [music] as well as Christian interests, right? Uh representing uh the Jewish interest [music] is something called Apac. Uh and they have a membership of about a 100,000, but they're very wealthy. There are many billionaires in this group. So this group has financial power. Jang's prediction gained even more attention because [music] two other parts of his forecast appeared to come true. Still, not everyone agrees with Jang's conclusions. Many military analysts argue that the United States [music] retains overwhelming technological superiority with advanced air [music] power, naval forces, and global alliances that Iran cannot match. Yet, even some experts warn that wars against deeply [music] entrenched regional powers can become long, unpredictable, and costly. History offers plenty of examples where powerful militaries struggled to achieve [music] their political goals. That is why Jang's prediction continues to spark debate. Is it a bold insight [music] into the limits of global power? Or simply a provocative theory that gained attention because events happened to move in the same direction? Right now, no one knows how the war will end. But one thing is clear. When historians, strategists, and ordinary viewers revisit that lecture from 2024, they are not just analyzing [music] a prediction. They are asking a much larger question. If the world's most powerful military enters a long war with Iran, what would victory actually look like?
